one_line_takeaway: STEEREDIT shows that compiling a Difference-in-Means compliance-direction steering vector into a null-space-constrained, trigger-gated weight update produces LLM supply-chain backdoors that sustain harmful completions (low Fallback Rate, e.g. 2.8% vs. baselines' 6.7-35.8% on Llama-3-8B-Instruct/StrongREJECT-family benchmarks) far more reliably than token-level affirmative-prefix backdoors, while retaining near-clean utility (URR consistently >97%) and partially resisting input-side/prompt-level defenses.
key_finding: On Llama-3-8B-Instruct with StrongREJECT, STEEREDIT achieves ASRw=53.5% vs. clean-model baseline ASRw=1.0%, while keeping ASRw/o=2.3% (close to clean-model ASRw/o=1.4%) and FR=2.8% (vs. clean-model FR=2.4%), and URR=98.6% (vs. DualEdit 95.7%, JailbreakEdit 70.1%, ROME 85.9%, SFT 81.2%). Across all 12 model-dataset combinations in Table 1, STEEREDIT achieves the lowest Fallback Rate among all methods (typically <15%, often <7%), demonstrating it converts superficial 'affirmative-prefix' compliance into sustained harmful completions rather than reverting to refusal -- the specific reliability gap the paper targets. Ablation (Table 3, Llama-3-8B-Instruct): removing the null-space constraint causes ASRw/o to jump from 2.3% to 63.7% and URR to drop from 98.6% to 61.2%, confirming the null-space projection -- not the steering vector alone -- is what produces stealthiness; the RV-Ablation baseline (direct refusal-direction ablation, no compilation/gating) achieves high ASRw (85.8%) but indiscriminately affects all inputs (ASRw/o=82.0%, URR=56.0%). Data efficiency: performance is stable with as few as 16 proxy samples (Figure 5). Against defenses (Table 4, Llama-3-8B-Instruct): under BEAT, the strongest tested defense, STEEREDIT's ASR is reduced to 10.6% but remains well above the clean model's 1.0%, and STEEREDIT's XSTest overrefusal after BEAT (2.2%) stays far lower than JailbreakEdit's (25.6%) or DualEdit's (4.8%), indicating STEEREDIT backdoors are both harder to fully suppress and less disruptive to benign behavior when a defense is applied.
core_contribution: The paper introduces STEEREDIT, a post-hoc weight-editing method for injecting trigger-gated 'jailbreak backdoors' into safety-aligned LLMs. Instead of the common approach of editing weights to force a token-level affirmative prefix (e.g. 'Sure'), which the authors show is unreliable because models often revert to refusal after the initial token, STEEREDIT compiles a compliance-direction steering vector (extracted via Difference-in-Means over hidden states of benign vs. harmful prompts) directly into a static low-rank weight perturbation at chosen MLP down-projection layers. The edit is gated by the trigger token via a null-space constraint: the update is restricted (via SVD-derived projection onto the left null space of clean/no-trigger key activations) to be zero on non-trigger inputs, so the backdoor is provably dormant on clean activations while shifting representations toward compliance whenever the trigger token's key vector is present. The optimal update has a closed-form regularized least-squares solution.
threat_model: Supply-chain attacker with write access to model parameters theta before final deployment (e.g. malicious third-party model provider, insider, or compromised intermediary distributing checkpoints via model hubs). The attacker can inject a perturbation delta (theta* = theta + delta) and has access to a small number of public proxy samples (benign prompts and harmful queries) sufficient to calibrate the attack, but does not control the inference pipeline at runtime (distinguishing this from live activation-steering attacks). Attacker goals, formalized in the paper: (1) effectiveness -- inputs containing harmful content plus the trigger token must elicit compliant (harmful) generation; (2) stealthiness -- on inputs without the trigger, the model must behave indistinguishably from the clean model (retain utility on benign inputs and continue refusing harmful inputs), to evade standard safety audits.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) the steering strength alpha is an explicit control knob but choosing it still requires a manual trade-off between attack strength and benign-behavior preservation; no automated calibration procedure adapting alpha to a target deployment distribution is provided. (2) The null-space projection relies on a finite benign reference set to estimate the activation subspace; distribution shift between this reference set and real-world benign inputs could weaken the non-interference guarantee, and no online/robust null-space estimation is explored. (3) Evaluation covers a limited set of models (3), datasets, and defenses (3); broader architectures, stronger/adaptive defenses, and more diverse safety policies are left to future work. (4) The paper uses a simple, single trigger design and compiles a single representation-level direction per attack; extending to more naturalistic triggers or multi-direction/task-conditional compilation is noted as future work. Additionally (reviewer-noted, grounded in the paper's own numbers, not the authors' text): even the best-performing model/dataset combination in Table 1 leaves ASRw well below 100% (e.g. 53.5%-85.4% depending on model/dataset), so the attack is not universally reliable; and defenses reduce but do not eliminate the backdoor (BEAT leaves 10.6% residual ASR versus a 1.0% clean baseline on Llama-3-8B-Instruct), so the method is not undefeatable, only harder to fully suppress than prior baselines.

datasets_used: Databricks Dolly (10,000 benign prompts, used for calibration/proxy dataset)
AdvBench (256 harmful queries sampled as proxy set for calibration)
XSTest (overrefusal rate, used only in defense experiments)
quantitative_results: Llama-3-8B-Instruct + STEEREDIT on StrongREJECT: ASRw=53.5%, ASRw/o=2.3%, FR=2.8% (clean model: ASRw=1.0%, ASRw/o=1.4%, FR=2.4%)
Llama-3-8B-Instruct + STEEREDIT on DAN: ASRw=83.3%, ASRw/o=32.1%, FR=5.1%
Llama-3-8B-Instruct utility: AlpacaEval=32.1, TruthfulQA=52.2, GSM-8K=74.0, URR=98.6% (base model: 33.2/52.2/74.6, URR=100)

Ablation on Llama-3-8B-Instruct (Table 3): STEEREDIT ASRw=53.5%/ASRw/o=2.3%/URR=98.6% vs. 'w/o Null-Space Constraint' ASRw=65.5%/ASRw/o=63.7%/URR=61.2% vs. RV-Ablation ASRw=85.8%/ASRw/o=82.0%/URR=56.0%

Defense results on Llama-3-8B-Instruct (Table 4): STEEREDIT clean ASR=53.5% -> under BEAT ASR=10.6% (XSTest overrefusal 2.2%); under ONION ASR=14.5% (XSTest 2.4%); under Self-Reminder ASR=13.9% (XSTest overrefusal 4.8%). Compare JailbreakEdit clean ASR=26.7% -> Self-Reminder ASR=6.9% but XSTest overrefusal spikes to 40.0%
Proxy dataset size ablation: STEEREDIT performance stable with as few as 16 proxy samples, diminishing returns beyond 64 (Figure 5)
Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct + STEEREDIT on Misuse: ASRw=67.3%, ASRw/o=13.1%, FR=5.4% (baseline DualEdit: ASRw=35.0%, ASRw/o=11.8%, FR=10.9%)
baselines_compared: ROME (Meng et al. 2022a) -- factual model-editing method adapted as a backdoor baseline
JailbreakEdit (Chen et al. 2024b) -- prior token-level affirmative-prefix weight-editing backdoor
DualEdit (Jiang et al. 2025) -- prior editing-based backdoor targeting safety fallback mitigation
SFT (supervised fine-tuning, per Hubinger et al. 2024 'Sleeper Agents' framing) -- data-poisoning-style baseline

RV-Ablation (Arditi et al. 2024) -- direct refusal-direction ablation applied indiscriminately to all inputs, used only in the ablation study to isolate the effect of trigger-gating/null-space compilation
